meta.title: Mobility & Joint Restoration Program
meta.subtitle: Personalized Training Protocol
meta.description: 8-week joint restoration program with 12-month skills roadmap. Rebuilds shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility — then progresses to muscle-ups, tuck planche, pistol squats, and handstands.
meta.author: AI Coach

sessions.title: Daily CARs
sessions.subtitle: Controlled Articular Rotations — 7-10 min
sessions.description: Do these every single day, no exceptions. CARs are the foundation — they maintain joint health, identify restrictions early, and prime your nervous system. Think of them as brushing your teeth for your joints.

exercises.name: Neck CARs
exercises.notes: Slow, controlled circles. Keep shoulders still. If you find a sticky spot, spend extra time there.

exercises.name: Shoulder CARs
exercises.notes: CRITICAL for you. Make fist, lock elbow straight, trace the biggest circle possible. Go SLOW — 10 seconds per rotation minimum. Pain-free range only, expand it over weeks.

exercises.name: Hip CARs (Standing)
exercises.notes: Hold a wall for balance. Drive knee up, open out, extend back, return. Keep pelvis still — the rotation happens at the hip socket only.

exercises.name: Ankle CARs
exercises.notes: CRITICAL — especially right side (Achilles repair). Seated, lift foot off ground, make slow full circles. Note any clicking or restriction without forcing through it.

exercises.name: Spine CARs (Segmental Cat-Cow)
exercises.notes: On all fours, roll through flexion and extension one vertebra at a time. Don't rush. This decompresses the spine after desk work.

exercises.name: Wrist CARs
exercises.notes: Make a fist, trace slow full circles at the wrist. You're doing planks, bear crawls, and bench pressing — your wrists take a beating. These also help you identify early warning signs of overuse before they become problems.

exercises.name: T-Spine Rotation (Quadruped)
exercises.notes: On all fours, place one hand behind your head. Rotate that elbow toward the ceiling, then down toward the opposite wrist. Follow your elbow with your eyes. This is the single most important addition for you — your desk-locked thoracic spine is the upstream bottleneck for your shoulders. If this doesn't rotate, your shoulder compensates during every press.

sessions.title: Session A: Shoulder Rehab & Stability
sessions.subtitle: 15-20 min — Do 2x per week
sessions.description: Your shoulders are compensating for weak scapular stabilizers and rotator cuff. This session rebuilds the foundation so your pressing strength can actually express itself without pain. Within 3-4 weeks, planks and overhead work should start feeling noticeably better.

exercises.name: Dead Hangs
exercises.notes: Use your pull-up bar. Passive hang, let your shoulders stretch toward your ears. This decompresses the shoulder joint and builds grip. If 30s is easy, progress to active hangs (pull shoulders down while hanging).

exercises.name: T-Spine Extension Over Foam Roller
exercises.notes: Place foam roller across your mid-back. Support your head with hands, elbows pointed forward. Extend back over the roller, then crunch forward — 10 reps. Then hold the extended position for 30 seconds. Move the roller up or down one vertebra and repeat. This directly unlocks the thoracic extension your shoulders need for overhead pressing and front rack position.

exercises.name: Open Book (Thread the Needle)
exercises.notes: Lie on your side, knees stacked and bent at 90°. Top arm opens toward the ceiling and behind you while keeping knees together. Follow your hand with your eyes. The rotation should come from your mid-back, NOT your lower back — keeping your knees stacked prevents lumbar rotation. This is the movement pattern that lets you actually rotate during daily life without shoulder or low back compensation.

exercises.name: Scapular Push-ups
exercises.notes: Plank position, arms locked straight. Only your shoulder blades move — protract (spread apart) and retract (squeeze together). This trains serratus anterior, which is likely weak and causing your plank shoulder pain.

exercises.name: Banded External Rotation at 90°
exercises.notes: Elbow at 90°, upper arm at shoulder height. Rotate forearm up against band resistance. Slow eccentric (3 sec lowering). This directly strengthens the rotator cuff in the position where it fails during pressing.

exercises.name: Prone Y-T-W Raises
exercises.notes: Lie face down on bench, light dumbbells (3-5 lb to start). Y = arms overhead in a V. T = arms straight out. W = elbows bent, squeeze shoulder blades. If 5 lb is too much, start bodyweight. These are humbling.

exercises.name: Wall Slides
exercises.notes: Back and arms flat against wall. Slide arms up overhead keeping everything in contact with the wall. If your lower back arches off the wall, that's a mobility restriction — only go as high as you can maintain contact.

exercises.name: Shoulder Sleeper Stretch with PAILs/RAILs
exercises.notes: Lie on your side, bottom arm at 90°. Use top hand to gently press into internal rotation. Hold 60s passive. Then PAILs: push bottom hand INTO the floor (10s, 20% effort building to 70%). Then RAILs: try to rotate hand UP without moving (10s same ramp). Re-stretch — you'll gain range.

exercises.name: Hollow Body Hold
exercises.notes: Lie on your back, arms overhead, legs extended. Press lower back INTO the floor and lift shoulders and feet slightly off the ground. If your low back peels off the floor, bend your knees or bring arms to your sides to regress. This is the missing link behind your plank shoulder pain — when your core can't hold position, your shoulders compensate. Build this, and planks and pressing improve.

sessions.title: Session B: Knee & Hip Mobility
sessions.subtitle: 15-20 min — Do 2x per week
sessions.description: Your lateral lunge pain and cross-legged sitting difficulty both point to tight hip adductors and weak VMO (inner quad). This session opens the hips and builds the knee stability that lets you load lateral movements safely. The Cossack squat is your eventual goal movement.

exercises.name: 90/90 Hip Switches
exercises.notes: Sit with both legs at 90°. Rotate from one side to the other. Keep spine tall. If you can't get your knees to the floor, that's fine — work the range you have. This is your hip mobility barometer.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge with 3-sec Hold
exercises.notes: Lie on back, knees bent, feet flat. Drive through heels, squeeze glutes HARD at the top, hold 3 seconds. Lower slowly. If you feel this in your hamstrings more than your glutes, bring your feet closer to your butt. This wakes up the glutes so they fire properly during squats, lunges, and walking. At 46 with desk work, your glutes are almost certainly underperforming — this fixes that.

exercises.name: Banded Clamshells
exercises.notes: Lie on your side, knees bent, band around knees. Open top knee against band resistance while keeping feet together. Slow and controlled — don't let the band snap your knee back down. This targets the gluteus medius, which stabilizes your pelvis and directly protects your knees during lateral movements. If your Cossack squats and side lunges hurt, weak glute med is likely a big contributor.

exercises.name: Tibialis Anterior Raises
exercises.notes: Stand with back against wall, feet ~12 inches out. Pull toes up toward shins, lower slowly. This strengthens the front of your shin and protects the knee. The Knees Over Toes Guy made this famous for good reason.

exercises.name: Banded Terminal Knee Extensions
exercises.notes: Attach band behind your knee to a low anchor. Stand facing away, slight bend. Squeeze to full lockout against band resistance. This isolates the VMO — the inner quad muscle that stabilizes the kneecap.

exercises.name: ATG Split Squat (Regressed)
exercises.notes: Start with hands on bench/chair for support. Back knee tracks all the way down and forward. ONLY go as deep as you can control without pain. Weeks 1-2: use support. Weeks 3-4: reduce support. This is the single best knee bulletproofing exercise.

Foundation.notes: Use bench support, partial depth only.

exercises.name: Cossack Squat (Partial ROM)
exercises.notes: Bodyweight only. Start with a small range — you do NOT need to go ass-to-grass. Hold a doorframe or rack for balance. This directly trains the lateral movement pattern that's giving your knees trouble. Depth will come with consistency.

Foundation.notes: Hold doorframe, go only as deep as comfortable.

Build.notes: Less support, aim for parallel thigh depth.

Load.notes: Goblet Cossack Squats with light dumbbell.

exercises.name: Active Frog Stretch
exercises.notes: On all fours, knees wide, rock forward and back. Keep spine neutral. This opens the adductors (inner thigh) which are contributing to both your lateral lunge pain and cross-legged sitting difficulty. Active rocking > static hold.

exercises.name: Copenhagen Plank
exercises.notes: Side plank position with top leg on a bench, bottom leg hanging free. This is a two-for-one — it trains lateral core strength AND adductor strength simultaneously. Directly addresses your lateral lunge knee pain. Regress by using bottom knee on the floor or shortening the hold. These are brutally hard at first — 10 seconds is fine to start.

sessions.title: Session C: Ankle/Achilles & Integration
sessions.subtitle: 15-20 min — Do 1-2x per week
sessions.description: Your repaired Achilles needs consistent eccentric loading to stay resilient — this is backed by decades of research (Alfredson protocol). Combined with dorsiflexion work, this session protects the ankle and unlocks deeper squat positions. The balance work re-trains proprioception that was disrupted by the surgery.

exercises.name: Extended Ankle CARs
exercises.notes: Slower and more deliberate than your daily CARs. Spend 5+ seconds per rotation on the right ankle. Map out the full range, note where it catches. Over weeks, the circle should get bigger.

exercises.name: Eccentric Calf Raises (Alfredson Protocol)
exercises.notes: CRITICAL for your Achilles. Stand on step edge, raise up on BOTH feet, then lower slowly (3-4 sec) on ONE foot. Do both straight-knee (gastroc) and bent-knee (soleus) versions. The right side may feel tight — stay in a pain-free range. This is the gold standard for Achilles tendon health.

Foundation.notes: Both-leg lower if single-leg is too intense on the right.

exercises.name: Wall Dorsiflexion Mobilization
exercises.notes: Face wall, foot ~4 inches away. Drive knee forward over toes, keeping heel DOWN. Measure progress by how far from the wall you can get your foot while still touching knee to wall. Your right side will be tighter — that's the Achilles repair limiting dorsiflexion.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Closed)
exercises.notes: Start with eyes open if needed. Bare feet on firm surface. The goal is rebuilding proprioception in the repaired ankle. If 30s is easy with eyes closed, try it on a folded towel for an unstable surface.

Load.notes: Single-leg balance on unstable surface (folded towel or pillow).

exercises.name: Supported Deep Squat Hold
exercises.notes: Hold a doorframe, squat rack, or heavy dumbbell as counterbalance. Sink as deep as comfortable, heels down. Let your hips open and spine lengthen. This integrates ankle, knee, and hip mobility into one functional position. If heels lift, elevate them on a plate or book.

exercises.name: Dead Bugs
exercises.notes: Lie on your back, arms up, knees at 90°. Press lower back into floor. Slowly extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. If your back arches, you've gone too far — shorten the range. This trains anti-extension and hip dissociation, directly improving your squat and overhead positions.

exercises.name: Bear Crawl
exercises.notes: Hands and feet on ground, knees hovering ~2 inches off floor. Crawl forward and backward slowly. This integrates shoulder stability, hip mobility, and ankle mobility under load in a way that mimics real-world movement. Keep breathing.

sessions.title: Session D: Core Stability
sessions.subtitle: 15-20 min — Do 1-2x per week
sessions.description: Your shoulders, knees, and ankles all depend on a stable trunk. When the core can't hold position, your joints compensate — that's a big part of why you keep getting injured. This session trains anti-extension, anti-rotation, and anti-lateral-flexion — the three pillars of functional core stability. These aren't crunches; they teach your core to resist unwanted movement so your limbs can move freely. Exercises marked with ◆ in other sessions are drawn from here.

exercises.name: Dead Bugs
exercises.notes: The king of core exercises for your situation. Lie on back, arms up, knees at 90°. Press lower back into floor — this is the cue that matters. Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. Slow and controlled — 3 seconds out, 3 seconds back. If your back arches, shorten the range. Progress by holding a light dumbbell overhead or adding a band around your feet.

exercises.name: Pallof Press
exercises.notes: Attach band to anchor at chest height. Stand sideways, hold band at chest. Press straight out and hold 2 seconds. The band tries to rotate you — your core resists. This anti-rotation strength directly supports your Cossack squats and lateral lunge stability. Keep hips square, don't let the band pull you.

exercises.name: Bird Dogs
exercises.notes: On all fours, extend opposite arm and leg simultaneously. Hold top position for 2 seconds. Keep hips LEVEL — the most common mistake is letting the hip drop on the lifting side. Place a water bottle on your lower back as feedback — if it falls, you're rotating too much. This trains the cross-body stabilization pattern used in walking, running, and all unilateral exercises.

exercises.name: Hollow Body Hold
exercises.notes: Arms overhead, legs extended, low back pressed into floor, shoulders and feet lifted. Scale it: bent knees with arms at sides (easiest) → straight legs with arms at sides → straight legs with arms overhead (hardest). If you can hold 30s at the hardest version, you can progress to hollow body rocks.

exercises.name: Copenhagen Plank
exercises.notes: Side plank with top leg on bench, bottom leg free. Trains lateral core and adductors simultaneously. Regression: bend bottom knee to floor, or use a shorter bench so you're supporting from the knee instead of the foot. Progression: add hip dips (lower and lift hips while holding). This exercise alone can fix a lot of your lateral movement issues.

exercises.name: Suitcase Carry
exercises.notes: Hold a single dumbbell at your side (moderate weight — 25-40 lb range), walk slowly. Keep your trunk PERFECTLY upright — don't lean away from the weight. This trains anti-lateral-flexion and grip strength simultaneously. You'll feel your obliques and deep stabilizers light up. Walk slowly and deliberately, don't rush.

sessions.title: Active Recovery
sessions.subtitle: 20-30 min — Do 1-2x per week
sessions.description: Easy effort only. The purpose is blood flow, not fitness. If you find yourself breathing hard on the bike, you're going too hard. Pair with foam rolling to address tissue quality.

exercises.name: Stationary Bike — Easy Spin
exercises.notes: Conversational pace. RPE 3-4 out of 10. Slight resistance. This drives blood flow to your knees and ankles without impact stress. Good for Achilles flare-up days too.

exercises.name: Foam Roll — Quads & IT Band
exercises.notes: Slow passes, pause on tender spots for 30s. Focus on the outer quad / IT band area — this feeds into your lateral lunge knee issue.

exercises.name: Foam Roll — Calves & Peroneals
exercises.notes: Roll the back AND sides of the calf. Extra attention on the right side around the Achilles area — not directly on the tendon, but the muscle above it.

exercises.name: Lacrosse Ball — Shoulder / Upper Back
exercises.notes: Pin ball between your back and the wall. Roll around the scapula (shoulder blade) area and posterior deltoid. Find the spots that refer sensation — those are the adhesions limiting your shoulder mobility.

weekTemplates.name: 4-Day (Minimum Effective)
weekTemplates.description: For busy weeks. Prioritizes the daily CARs habit and hits each problem area once. Core is covered by the ◆ exercises integrated into each session.

days.note: Strength training day — do CARs as warmup

weekTemplates.name: 5-Day (Recommended)
weekTemplates.description: Optimal balance. Each focus area gets proper attention plus a dedicated core session. Core exercises marked ◆ in other sessions give you additional core volume throughout the week.

days.note: Core + strength training — CARs as warmup, Session D, then your lifting
Strength training — CARs as warmup + add 2-3 Session B exercises as prehab

weekTemplates.name: 6-Day (Accelerated)
weekTemplates.description: For when you're in a groove. Dedicated core session plus ◆ exercises throughout the week give you 3-4 core hits total.

days.note: Core day — pair with strength training if time allows

progressions.focus: Learn the movements. Don't chase depth or load.
details: CARs: 70% effort — find your current range, don't push past it
All exercises at bodyweight or lightest band/dumbbell
ATG Split Squat: use bench support, partial depth only
Cossack Squat: hold doorframe, go only as deep as comfortable
Eccentric calf raises: both-leg lower if single-leg is too intense on the right
Dead hangs: aim for 15-20s, build to 30s
Core: Dead bugs with bent knees, hollow body with knees bent and arms at sides, Copenhagen plank from knees (10-15s)

progressions.focus: Add range and time under tension. Reduce assistance.
details: CARs: 85% effort — actively push into end ranges
Increase band resistance one level on external rotations and TKEs
ATG Split Squat: reduce bench support, increase depth
Cossack Squat: less support, aim for parallel thigh depth
Eccentric calf raises: full single-leg protocol
Dead hangs: progress to active hangs (depress shoulders while hanging)
Add 5-10s to all timed holds
Core: Dead bugs with straight legs, hollow body with legs extended, Copenhagen plank full (20-30s), add Pallof press with medium band

progressions.focus: Start adding external load to mobility positions.
details: CARs: full effort with irradiation (squeeze fist, brace core)
Goblet Cossack Squats with light dumbbell
ATG Split Squat with light dumbbells, no support
Weighted deep squat holds (goblet position)
Y-T-W raises: increase dumbbell weight by 2-3 lb
Begin testing overhead press and front squat with improved positions
Single-leg balance on unstable surface (folded towel or pillow)
Core: Hollow body rocks, Copenhagen plank with hip dips, suitcase carries at 30-40 lb, dead bugs with band resistance

roadmap.name: Phase 1: Joint Restoration
roadmap.summary: This is the program you're in now. Rebuild shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility. No skill work yet — your joints aren't ready.
roadmap.keyFocus: The temptation will be to start skill work early. Don't. Every week of foundation you invest here saves you months of plateau and re-injury later. If you fail any gate test at week 8, extend Phase 1 by 2-week increments until you pass.

roadmap.name: Phase 2: Strength Prerequisites
roadmap.summary: Build the raw strength and positions your skills require. Shoulder strength, straight-arm conditioning, single-leg loading, and wrist preparation. Daily CARs and core work continue as maintenance.
roadmap.keyFocus: This phase is dominated by pull-up volume, dip volume, and straight-arm conditioning. If you're doing BODi, use it for lower body days and replace upper body BODi with the skill-prep work. The false grip and straight-arm work will feel completely foreign — your tendons need time to adapt.

roadmap.name: Phase 3: Skill Acquisition
roadmap.summary: Active skill practice begins. Muscle-up transition training, planche lean progressions, pistol squat depth development, and handstand balance work. This is as much neurological as physical.
roadmap.keyFocus: Skill days replace some strength days. You're now practicing 3-4 skills per week alongside maintenance mobility and strength. The muscle-up will likely click before the planche — the transition is a technique breakthrough moment. Film every attempt.

roadmap.name: Phase 4: Refinement & Beyond
roadmap.summary: Polish skill quality, add reps, and begin harder progressions. Multiple clean muscle-ups, advanced planche variations, loaded pistol squats, freestanding handstand confidence.
roadmap.keyFocus: This becomes your ongoing training life. Rotate focus between skills based on interest and progress. The planche journey continues well beyond a year — enjoy the process. The muscle-up becomes a warm-up exercise you can rep out.

skills.name: Muscle Up
skills.currentState: 3-5 pull-ups (previously 7-10 before shoulder issues). No muscle-up experience. Shoulder pain during planks and pressing.
skills.requirements: 12+ strict pull-ups, 15+ dips, false grip strength, explosive pulling power, deep shoulder extension under load, and the transition technique.
skills.gapAnalysis: Your pulling strength base exists but is suppressed by shoulder instability. Once shoulders stabilize (Phase 1), pull-up numbers will rebound quickly since the muscle memory is there. The false grip and transition are completely new skills. The deep shoulder extension required at the transition point is the riskiest part given your shoulder history — this is why we must not rush.
skills.timeline: First muscle-up: 6-9 months (weeks 24-36). Confident reps: 9-12 months.

milestones.name: 8-10 strict pull-ups
milestones.description: Rebuild pulling base after shoulder stabilization

milestones.name: False grip hang 20+ sec
milestones.description: Wrist and forearm conditioning for transition

milestones.name: High pull-ups to sternum
milestones.description: Explosive pulling height needed for transition

skills.name: Tuck Planche
skills.currentState: No planche training history. Shoulder pain limits plank position. Scapular stabilizer weakness identified.
skills.requirements: Strong scapular protraction, straight-arm pressing strength, progressive lean tolerance, conditioned wrists. The tuck planche is the most achievable planche variation and still looks impressive — hips level with shoulders, knees tucked to chest, body floating on hands.
skills.gapAnalysis: The tuck planche requires straight-arm pressing with protracted scapulae that standard lifting doesn't build. Your shoulders need to be fully stable before you start leaning. The good news: tuck planche is a realistic 9-15 month goal, well within the same timeline as your muscle-up. Advanced tuck (legs partially extended) is the stretch goal beyond that.
skills.timeline: Planche lean: 4-6 months. Frog stand: 3-4 months. Tuck planche (2-3 sec): 9-12 months. Tuck planche (5-10 sec): 12-15 months. Advanced tuck planche: 15-24 months (stretch goal).

skills.name: Pistol Squat
skills.currentState: Limited depth on pistol squats. Lateral lunge knee pain. Surgically repaired right Achilles limiting dorsiflexion. Tight hips.
skills.requirements: Full ankle dorsiflexion (especially right side), strong VMO and quad, hip flexor strength to hold the non-working leg extended, single-leg balance, and hip mobility to sit deep without butt wink.
skills.gapAnalysis: Your three limiters are exactly what Phase 1 targets: ankle dorsiflexion, knee stability, and hip mobility. The specific gap is single-leg strength and hip flexor endurance. Your right side will always be harder due to the Achilles — accept asymmetry and work both sides to their respective maximums.
skills.timeline: Pistol to box (16 inch): 8-12 weeks. Pistol to box (8 inch): 16-20 weeks. Full-depth pistol (both legs): 5-8 months. Right leg may lag 2-4 weeks behind left.

skills.name: Handstand
skills.currentState: Never achieved a handstand. Shoulder pain during overhead loading. Limited thoracic extension. Core work developing.
skills.requirements: Full overhead shoulder mobility, conditioned wrists, scapular elevation strength, proprioceptive balance, and extensive wall practice before going freestanding.
skills.gapAnalysis: Your t-spine restriction and shoulder instability are the current blockers. Once those resolve (Phase 1-2), the handstand becomes primarily a balance skill. At 46, balance adaptation is slower but absolutely happens — expect more time in wall-supported positions. Your core strength from Session D will transfer directly.
skills.timeline: Wall handstand 30 sec: 3-4 months. Wall handstand 60 sec with controlled entry: 5-6 months. Freestanding 5-10 sec: 6-9 months. Freestanding 15-30 sec: 9-15 months.

benchmarks.name: Wall Dorsiflexion Test
benchmarks.howTo: Face wall, foot back from wall. Drive knee forward keeping heel down. Measure max distance (inches) where knee touches wall and heel stays down.
benchmarks.target: Left: 4-5 inches. Right (Achilles): will start lower — track improvement, don't compare sides.

benchmarks.name: Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Closed)
benchmarks.howTo: Stand on one foot, close eyes, time how long before you touch down or open eyes.
benchmarks.target: 30 seconds each side. Right ankle will lag — that's expected.

benchmarks.name: Hollow Body Hold Time
benchmarks.howTo: Full position (arms overhead, legs straight). Time until low back lifts off floor.

benchmarks.name: Overhead Reach (Wall Test)
benchmarks.howTo: Stand with back, butt, and head against wall. Raise arms overhead trying to touch thumbs to wall without arching your low back off the wall.
benchmarks.target: Thumbs touch wall with back flat = good t-spine and shoulder mobility. If back arches, that's your t-spine restriction.

benchmarks.name: Deep Squat Assessment
benchmarks.howTo: Feet shoulder width, squat as deep as possible without any support. Note: heels lifting, excessive forward lean, knees caving, inability to keep chest up.
benchmarks.target: Hips below parallel, heels down, chest up, no pain. Use a phone to film yourself — you'll miss compensations otherwise.
benchmarks.frequency: Test day 1, film it. Re-film every 4 weeks.

benchmarks.name: 90/90 Hip Test
benchmarks.howTo: Sit in 90/90 position (both legs at 90°). Can you sit tall with both knees touching or near the floor?
benchmarks.target: Both knees within 3-4 inches of floor, spine upright without hands for support.

benchmarks.name: Cross-Body Shoulder Reach
benchmarks.howTo: Reach right hand behind your back from below, left hand from above. How close can your fingertips get? Repeat on the other side.
benchmarks.target: Fingers touching or overlapping. Note the difference between sides — your tighter side is the priority.

benchmarks.name: Pain-Free Planks
benchmarks.howTo: Hold a standard plank position. Note any shoulder pain during or after.

benchmarks.name: Strict Pull-ups (Phase 1 Gate)
benchmarks.howTo: Dead hang start, pull chin over bar, full lockout between reps. No kipping.

benchmarks.name: Clean Dips (Phase 1 Gate)
benchmarks.howTo: Parallel bars or bench dips, full range of motion, controlled tempo.

benchmarks.name: Cossack Squat Full Depth
benchmarks.howTo: Full-depth bodyweight Cossack squat each side, no external support.
benchmarks.target: Full depth both sides, no support, no pain.

benchmarks.name: Single-Leg Balance 30s (Gate)
benchmarks.howTo: Stand on one foot, close eyes. Time until loss of balance.

benchmarks.name: Wall Dorsiflexion 4+ Inches
benchmarks.howTo: Face wall, drive knee forward. Measure distance from wall where heel stays down.

benchmarks.name: Hollow Body Hold 30s (Gate)
benchmarks.howTo: Arms overhead, legs straight. Hold until low back lifts off floor.

benchmarks.name: Overhead Wall Test (Gate)
benchmarks.howTo: Back against wall, raise arms overhead keeping back flat.
benchmarks.target: Thumbs touch wall, back stays flat.

benchmarks.name: Strict Pull-ups (Phase 2 Gate)
benchmarks.howTo: Dead hang start, strict form, full range.

benchmarks.name: Parallel Bar Dips (Phase 2 Gate)
benchmarks.howTo: Parallel bars, full depth, controlled tempo.

benchmarks.name: False Grip Hang
benchmarks.howTo: Hang from bar or rings with false grip (wrist over bar). Time the hold.

benchmarks.name: Ring Support Hold
benchmarks.howTo: Support position on rings, shoulders externally rotated (rings turned out).

benchmarks.name: Pistol Squat to Box
benchmarks.howTo: Stand on one leg, lower to bench/box (12-16 inch height), stand back up.
benchmarks.target: Controlled descent and ascent each leg.

benchmarks.name: Wall Handstand Hold
benchmarks.howTo: Stomach facing wall, nose almost touching. Hold inverted position.

benchmarks.name: Planche Lean
benchmarks.howTo: Hands on floor, lean forward shifting weight over hands at 20+ degree angle.

benchmarks.name: Scapular Pull-ups
benchmarks.howTo: From dead hang, depress shoulders (pull shoulder blades down), return to passive hang.

benchmarks.name: Muscle-ups (Phase 3 Gate)
benchmarks.howTo: From dead hang, pull up and transition over the bar or rings to support position.

benchmarks.name: Tuck Planche Hold
benchmarks.howTo: Hands on floor or parallettes, tuck knees to chest, lift hips level with shoulders.

benchmarks.name: Full Pistol Squat
benchmarks.howTo: Single-leg squat to full depth. Counterbalance with a light weight is acceptable.

benchmarks.name: Freestanding Handstand
benchmarks.howTo: Kick up to handstand in open space. Hold without wall support.
benchmarks.target: 20-second hold OR 45-second wall handstand with controlled entry/exit.

benchmarks.name: Negative Muscle-up
benchmarks.howTo: Start in support position on bar, lower slowly through transition to hang.

benchmarks.name: Tuck Planche (Hips Level)
benchmarks.howTo: Tuck planche with hips at shoulder height, back flat.
benchmarks.target: Hips level with shoulders, held position.

benchmarks.name: Consecutive Muscle-ups
benchmarks.howTo: Multiple muscle-ups in a row without dismounting.

benchmarks.name: Tuck Planche 10s Hold
benchmarks.howTo: Tuck planche position held for duration.

benchmarks.name: Freestanding Handstand 15s
benchmarks.howTo: Freestanding handstand held for duration.

benchmarks.name: Full Pistol Squat (No Counterbalance)
benchmarks.howTo: Full-depth single-leg squat, arms free (no weight held for balance).
benchmarks.target: Full pistol each leg, no counterbalance.

benchmarks.name: Advanced Tuck Planche
benchmarks.howTo: Tuck planche with legs partially extended from tuck position.

foundations.title: Breathing & Bracing Protocol
foundations.description: Proper breathing and bracing is the foundation of all loaded movement. Master this before adding intensity to any exercise.

steps.name: Find Your Diaphragm
steps.instructions: Lie on your back, knees bent. Place one hand on your chest, one on your belly. Breathe in through your nose — ONLY the belly hand should rise. Practice until you can take 10 breaths where only your belly moves.

steps.name: 360 Breathing
steps.instructions: Place hands on your sides. When you inhale, your belly, sides, AND lower back should all expand outward like inflating a cylinder. Practice 10 breaths in the 90/90 position.

steps.name: Bracing Under Load
steps.instructions: Take a 360 breath in. Without exhaling, tighten your midsection as if someone is about to punch you in the gut. Inhale at the easy part, brace, execute the hard part, exhale at the top.

foundations.practice: Do 10 diaphragmatic breaths before daily CARs for the first 2 weeks.
